title: The efficacy and safety of β-nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation in healthy middle-aged adults: a randomized, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, dose-dependent clinical trial.
abstract: In animal studies, β-nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation increases nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) concentrations and improves healthspan and lifespan with great safety. However, it is unclear if these effects can be transferred to humans. This randomized, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, dose-dependent clinical trial included 80 middle-aged healthy adults being randomized for a 60-day clinical trial with once daily oral dosing of placebo, 300 mg, 600 mg, or 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide. The primary objective was to evaluate blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration with dose-dependent regimens. The secondary objectives were to assess the safety and tolerability of nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation, next to the evaluation of clinical efficacy by measuring physical performance (six-minute walking test), blood biological age (Aging.Ai 3.0 calculator), Homeostatic Model Assessment for Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR), and subjective general health assessment [36-Item Short Form Survey Instrument (SF-36)]. Statistical analysis was performed using the Per Protocol analysis with significant level set at p = 0.05. All 80 participants completed the trial without trial protocol violation. Blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations were statistically significantly increased among all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups at day 30 and day 60 when compared to both placebo and baseline (all p ≤ 0.001). Blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations were highest in the groups taking 600 mg and 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide. No safety issues, based on monitoring adverse events (AEs), laboratory and clinical measures, were found, and nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation was well tolerated. Walking distance increase during the six-minute walking test was statistically significantly higher in the 300 mg, 600 mg, and 900 mg groups compared to placebo at both days 30 and 60 (all p < 0.01), with longest walking distances measured in the 600 mg and 900 mg groups. The blood biological age increased significantly in the placebo group and stayed unchanged in all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups at day 60, which resulted in a significant difference between the treated groups and placebo (all p < 0.05). The HOMA-IR showed no statistically significant differences for all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups as compared to placebo at day 60. The change of SF-36 scores at day 30 and day 60 indicated statistically significantly better health of all three treated groups when compared to the placebo group (p < 0.05), except for the SF-36 score change in the 300 mg group at day 30. nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation increases blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations and is safe and well tolerated with oral dosing up to 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide daily. Clinical efficacy expressed by blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration and physical performance reaches highest at a dose of 600 mg daily oral intake. This trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT04823260, and Clinical Trial Registry - India, CTRI/2021/03/032421.

However, it is unclear if these effects can be transferred to humans.

This randomized, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group, dose-dependent clinical trial included 80 middle-aged healthy adults being randomized for a 60-day clinical trial with once daily oral dosing of placebo, 300 mg, 600 mg, or 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide.

Statistical analysis was performed using the Per Protocol analysis with significant level set at p = 0.05.
All 80 participants completed the trial without trial protocol violation.

Blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations were statistically significantly increased among all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups at day 30 and day 60 when compared to both placebo and baseline (all p ≤ 0.001).
Blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations were highest in the groups taking 600 mg and 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide.
No safety issues, based on monitoring adverse events (AEs), laboratory and clinical measures, were found, and nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation was well tolerated.

The blood biological age increased significantly in the placebo group and stayed unchanged in all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups at day 60, which resulted in a significant difference between the treated groups and placebo (all p < 0.05).
The HOMA-IR showed no statistically significant differences for all nicotinamide mononucleotide-treated groups as compared to placebo at day 60.

The change of SF-36 scores at day 30 and day 60 indicated statistically significantly better health of all three treated groups when compared to the placebo group (p < 0.05), except for the SF-36 score change in the 300 mg group at day 30. nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation increases blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentrations and is safe and well tolerated with oral dosing up to 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide daily.
Clinical efficacy expressed by blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration and physical performance reaches highest at a dose of 600 mg daily oral intake.
This trial was registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT04823260, and Clinical Trial Registry - India, CTRI/2021/03/032421.

title: Nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) as an anti-aging health product - Promises and safety concerns.
abstract: Elderly population has been progressively rising in the world, thus the demand for anti-aging heath products to assure longevity as well as to ameliorate age-related complications is also on the rise. Among various anti-aging health products, nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) has been gaining attentions of the consumers and the scientific community. This article intends to provide an overview on the current knowledge on promises and safety concerns of nicotinamide mononucleotide as an anti-aging health product. Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) levels in the body deplete with aging and it is associated with downregulation of energy production in mitochondria, oxidative stress, DNA damage, cognitive impairment and inflammatory conditions. However, nicotinamide mononucleotide, as the precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , can slow down this process by elevating nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels in the body. A number of in vivo studies have indicated affirmative results of therapeutic effects for various age-induced complications with nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation. One preclinical and one clinical study have been conducted to investigate the safety concerns of nicotinamide mononucleotide administration while a few more human clinical trials are being conducted. As there is a large influx of nicotinamide mononucleotide based anti-aging products on the market, proper clinical investigations are urgently needed to find out the effectiveness and safety of nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation.

This article intends to provide an overview on the current knowledge on promises and safety concerns of nicotinamide mononucleotide as an anti-aging health product.

Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) levels in the body deplete with aging and it is associated with downregulation of energy production in mitochondria, oxidative stress, DNA damage, cognitive impairment and inflammatory conditions.
However, nicotinamide mononucleotide, as the precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , can slow down this process by elevating nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels in the body.
A number of in vivo studies have indicated affirmative results of therapeutic effects for various age-induced complications with nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation.
One preclinical and one clinical study have been conducted to investigate the safety concerns of nicotinamide mononucleotide administration while a few more human clinical trials are being conducted.

As there is a large influx of nicotinamide mononucleotide based anti-aging products on the market, proper clinical investigations are urgently needed to find out the effectiveness and safety of nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation.

title: Towards personalized nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) concentration.
abstract: Nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) is a precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide), which declines with age. Supplementation of nicotinamide mononucleotide has been shown to improve blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration. However, the optimal nicotinamide mononucleotide dose remains unclear. This is a post-hoc analysis of a double-blinded clinical trial involving 80 generally healthy adults aged 40-65 years. The participants received a placebo or daily 300 mg, 600 mg, or 900 mg nicotinamide mononucleotide for 60 days. Blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration, blood biological age, homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance, 6-minute walk test, and 36-item short-form survey (SF-36) were measured at baseline and after supplement. A significant dose-dependent increase in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration change (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide Δ ) was observed following nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation, with a large coefficient of variation (29.2-113.3%) within group. The increase in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide Δ was associated with an improvement in the walking distance of 6-minute walk test and the SF-36 score. The median effect dose of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide Δ for the 6-minute walk test and SF-36 score was 15.7 nmol/L (95% CI: 10.9-20.5 nmol/L) and 13.5 nmol/L (95% CI; 10.5-16.5 nmol/L), respectively. Because of the high interindividual variability of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide Δ after nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation, monitoring nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration can provide valuable insights for tailoring personalized dosage regimens and optimizing nicotinamide mononucleotide utilization.

title: Beta-Hydroxybutyrate but not nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation mimics caloric restriction reducing early mortality in Daphnia.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + homeostasis is an important determinant of lifespan and may be a key mechanism of caloric restriction (CR) expansion of lifespan. Ketone bodies such as beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) that regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + abundance and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors such nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide), are known to extend life in experimental animals and ameliorate age-related conditions in humans. We tested the hypothesis that chronic BHB and nicotinamide mononucleotide exposure can extend lifespan similarly to the effect of CR treatment in a model organism Daphnia, a freshwater zooplankton crustacean. We also measured fecundity, lipofuscin accumulation, and lipid investments into offspring in Daphnia fed the full diet, full diet with BHB, nicotinamide mononucleotide, and combined treatments, and fed the CR diet (25% of the full diet). We show that BHB exposure, but not nicotinamide mononucleotide exposure, reduces early life mortality in fully fed Daphnia to levels similar to those observed under CR without compromising fecundity. We also observed that in a combined exposure cohort, nicotinamide mononucleotide nearly eliminates the beneficial effect of BHB. None of the treatments affected lipofuscin accumulation, but the nicotinamide mononucleotide and the combined treatment mimicked the effect of CR on neonate size in older females. An RNAseq experiment comparing the two diets and the two exposure treatments showed showed that BHB-treated Daphnia change expression of a variety of genes, including genes with known longevity extending effects, but differential expression of few genes is consistent with the effects of CR and their functionality is not clear.

title: Nicotinamide Mononucleotide Supplementation: Understanding Metabolic Variability and Clinical Implications.
abstract: Recent years have seen a surge in research focused on nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ decline and potential interventions, and despite significant progress, new discoveries continue to highlight the complexity of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ biology. Nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide), a well-established nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ precursor, has garnered considerable interest due to its capacity to elevate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ levels and induce promising health benefits in preclinical models. Clinical trials investigating nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation have yielded variable outcomes while shedding light on the intricacies of nicotinamide mononucleotide metabolism and revealing the critical roles played by gut microbiota and specific cellular uptake pathways. Individual variability in factors such as lifestyle, health conditions, genetics, and gut microbiome composition likely contributes to the observed discrepancies in clinical trial results. Preliminary evidence suggests that nicotinamide mononucleotide's effects may be context-dependent, varying based on a person's physiological state. Understanding these nuances is critical for definitively assessing the impact of manipulating nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ levels through nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation. Here, we review nicotinamide mononucleotide metabolism, focusing on current knowledge, pinpointing key areas where further research is needed, and outlining future directions to advance our understanding of its potential clinical significance.

title: Potential Synergistic Supplementation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ Promoting Compounds as a Strategy for Increasing Healthspan.
abstract: Disrupted biological function, manifesting through the hallmarks of aging, poses one of the largest threats to healthspan and risk of disease development, such as metabolic disorders, cardiovascular ailments, and neurodegeneration. In recent years, numerous geroprotectors, senolytics, and other nutraceuticals have emerged as potential disruptors of aging and may be viable interventions in the immediate state of human longevity science. In this review, we focus on the decrease in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+) with age and the supplementation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ precursors, such as nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) or nicotinamide riboside (NR), in combination with other geroprotective compounds, to restore nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ levels present in youth. Furthermore, these geroprotectors may enhance the efficacy of nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation while concurrently providing their own numerous health benefits. By analyzing the prevention of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ degradation through the inhibition of CD38 or supporting protective downstream agents of SIRT1, we provide a potential framework of the CD38/nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+/SIRT1 axis through which geroprotectors may enhance the efficacy of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ precursor supplementation and reduce the risk of age-related diseases, thereby potentiating healthspan in humans.

title: Excess glucose alone depress young mesenchymal stromal/stem cell osteogenesis and mitochondria activity within hours/days via nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + /SIRT1 axis.
abstract: The impact of global overconsumption of simple sugars on bone health, which peaks in adolescence/early adulthood and correlates with osteoporosis (OP) and fracture risk decades, is unclear. Mesenchymal stromal/stem cells (MSCs) are the progenitors of osteoblasts/bone-forming cells, and known to decrease their osteogenic differentiation capacity with age. Alarmingly, while there is correlative evidence that adolescents consuming greatest amounts of simple sugars have the lowest bone mass, there is no mechanistic understanding on the causality of this correlation. Bioinformatics analyses for energetics pathways involved during MSC differentiation using human cell information was performed. In vitro dissection of normal versus high glucose (HG) conditions on osteo-/adipo-lineage commitment and mitochondrial function was assessed using multi-sources of non-senescent human and murine MSCs; for in vivo validation, young mice was fed normal or HG-added water with subsequent analyses of bone marrow CD45 - MSCs. Bioinformatics analyses revealed mitochondrial and glucose-related metabolic pathways as integral to MSC osteo-/adipo-lineage commitment. Functionally, in vitro HG alone without differentiation induction decreased both MSC mitochondrial activity and osteogenesis while enhancing adipogenesis by 8 h' time due to depletion of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ), a vital mitochondrial co-enzyme and co-factor to Sirtuin (SIRT) 1, a longevity gene also involved in osteogenesis. In vivo, HG intake in young mice depleted MSC nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , with oral nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursor supplementation rapidly reversing both mitochondrial decline and osteo-/adipo-commitment in a SIRT1-dependent fashion within 1 ~ 5 days. We found a surprisingly rapid impact of excessive glucose, a single dietary factor, on MSC SIRT1 function and osteogenesis in youthful settings, and the crucial role of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -a single molecule-on both MSC mitochondrial function and lineage commitment. These findings have strong implications on future global OP and disability risks in light of current worldwide overconsumption of simple sugars.

title: Association between blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels and blood laboratory parameters at baseline and after nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation in middle-aged healthy individuals: post hoc analysis of a randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial.
abstract: To assess associations between blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) levels and laboratory parameters at baseline and after 60 days of nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation. Post hoc analysis of a randomized, double-blind, clinical trial of daily nicotinamide mononucleotide (300, 600, or 900 mg) or placebo in healthy middle-aged participants. Among the 80 participants (49.4 ± 6.8 year), the baseline nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide was 7.21 [5.5, 10.6] nM. Every 1 nM higher baseline nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide level was associated with 0.24% (95%CI: 0.05-0.44) higher lymphocytes, - 0.36% (95% CI: - 0.57 to - 0.14) lower neutrophils, 0.023% (95% CI: 0.011-0.036) higher triglycerides, - 0.009% (95% CI: - 0.015 to - 0.003) lower high-density lipoprotein, 0.02% (95% CI: 0.004-0.035) higher alanine transaminase, 0.01% (95% CI: 0.0003-0.021) aspartate transaminase. Every 1 nM increase in the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide level was associated with an increase in hemoglobin of 0.027% (95% CI: 0.01-0.045), red blood cells (RBC) of 0.025% (95% CI: 0.009-0.042), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 0.016 g/dL (95% CI: 0.008-0.025), and uric acid of 0.02% (95%CI: 0.003-0.038). Higher baseline nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels are associated with inflammatory, lipid, and liver profiles. nicotinamide mononucleotide-induced increases in blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide are associated with an increase in RBC parameters, potentially indicating enhanced oxygen-carrying capacity.
sentences: To assess associations between blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) levels and laboratory parameters at baseline and after 60 days of nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation.
Post hoc analysis of a randomized, double-blind, clinical trial of daily nicotinamide mononucleotide (300, 600, or 900 mg) or placebo in healthy middle-aged participants.

Every 1 nM increase in the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide level was associated with an increase in hemoglobin of 0.027% (95% CI: 0.01-0.045), red blood cells (RBC) of 0.025% (95% CI: 0.009-0.042), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 0.016 g/dL (95% CI: 0.008-0.025), and uric acid of 0.02% (95%CI: 0.003-0.038).

Higher baseline nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels are associated with inflammatory, lipid, and liver profiles. nicotinamide mononucleotide-induced increases in blood nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide are associated with an increase in RBC parameters, potentially indicating enhanced oxygen-carrying capacity.

title: Targeting nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + Metabolism as Interventions for Mitochondrial Disease.
abstract: Leigh syndrome is a mitochondrial disease characterized by neurological disorders, metabolic abnormality and premature death. There is no cure for Leigh syndrome; therefore, new therapeutic targets are urgently needed. In Ndufs4-KO mice, a mouse model of Leigh syndrome, we found that Complex I deficiency led to declines in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + redox imbalance. We tested the hypothesis that elevation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels would benefit Ndufs4-KO mice. Administration of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursor, nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) extended lifespan of Ndufs4-KO mice and attenuated lactic acidosis. nicotinamide mononucleotide increased lifespan by normalizing nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + redox imbalance and lowering HIF1a accumulation in Ndufs4-KO skeletal muscle without affecting the brain. nicotinamide mononucleotide up-regulated alpha-ketoglutarate (KG) levels in Ndufs4-KO muscle, a metabolite essential for HIF1a degradation. To test whether supplementation of KG can treat Ndufs4-KO mice, a cell-permeable KG, dimethyl ketoglutarate (DMKG) was administered. DMKG extended lifespan of Ndufs4-KO mice and delayed onset of neurological phenotype. This study identified therapeutic mechanisms that can be targeted pharmacologically to treat Leigh syndrome.

There is no cure for Leigh syndrome; therefore, new therapeutic targets are urgently needed.

To test whether supplementation of KG can treat Ndufs4-KO mice, a cell-permeable KG, dimethyl ketoglutarate (DMKG) was administered.

title: β-Nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation prolongs the lifespan of prematurely aged mice and protects colon function in ageing mice.
abstract: Ageing is defined as the degeneration of physiological functions in numerous tissues and organs of an organism, which occurs with age. As we age, the gut undergoes a series of changes and weaknesses that may contribute to overall ageing. Emerging evidence suggests that β-nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) plays a role in regulating intestinal function, but there is still a lack of literature on its role in maintaining the colon health of ageing mice. In our research, Zmpste24 -/- mice proved that nicotinamide mononucleotide prolonged their life span and delayed senescence. This study was designed to investigate the effects of long-term intervention on regulating colon function in ageing mice. Our results indicated that nicotinamide mononucleotide improved the pathology of intestinal epithelial cells and intestinal permeability by upregulating the expression of intestinal tight junction proteins and the number of goblet cells, increasing the release of anti-inflammatory factors, and increasing beneficial intestinal bacteria. nicotinamide mononucleotide increased the expression of the proteins SIRT1, NMNAT2, and NMNAT3 and decreased the expression of the protein P53. It also regulated the activity of ISCs by increasing Wnt/β-catenin and Lgr5. Our findings also revealed that nicotinamide mononucleotide caused a significant increase in the relative abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila and Bifidobacterium pseudolongum and notable differences in metabolic pathways related to choline metabolism in cancer. In summary, nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation can delay frailty in old age, aid healthy ageing, and delay gut ageing.

As we age, the gut undergoes a series of changes and weaknesses that may contribute to overall ageing.

Emerging evidence suggests that β-nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) plays a role in regulating intestinal function, but there is still a lack of literature on its role in maintaining the colon health of ageing mice.
In our research, Zmpste24 -/- mice proved that nicotinamide mononucleotide prolonged their life span and delayed senescence.
This study was designed to investigate the effects of long-term intervention on regulating colon function in ageing mice.

Our results indicated that nicotinamide mononucleotide improved the pathology of intestinal epithelial cells and intestinal permeability by upregulating the expression of intestinal tight junction proteins and the number of goblet cells, increasing the release of anti-inflammatory factors, and increasing beneficial intestinal bacteria. nicotinamide mononucleotide increased the expression of the proteins SIRT1, NMNAT2, and NMNAT3 and decreased the expression of the protein P53.
It also regulated the activity of ISCs by increasing Wnt/β-catenin and Lgr5.

Our findings also revealed that nicotinamide mononucleotide caused a significant increase in the relative abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila and Bifidobacterium pseudolongum and notable differences in metabolic pathways related to choline metabolism in cancer.
In summary, nicotinamide mononucleotide supplementation can delay frailty in old age, aid healthy ageing, and delay gut ageing.

title: Xanthine oxidoreductase knockout mice with high HPRT activity were not rescued by nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + replenishment.
abstract: Although xanthinuria is nonfatal in human, xanthine oxidoreductase knockout ( Xor- KO) mice have only a short lifespan. Hypoxanthine phosphoribosyltransferase activity (HPRT) in human and wild mice is higher than in laboratory mice. The aim of this study was to investigate the underlying mechanisms that give rise to the longer lifespan of high-HPRT/ Xor- KO mice. Before Xor -KO mice die, urinary excretion of hypoxanthine increased with a corresponding decrease in excretion of xanthine. The switch of excretion from xanthine to hypoxanthine might be a cause of death for Xor- KO mice, suggesting inhibition of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent IMP dehydrogenase. Because hypoxanthine inhibits the synthesis of nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide), a precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , the accumulation of hypoxanthine in Xor- KO mice may cause a depletion in the levels of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + . Moreover, urinary excretion of urate in high-HPRT/Uox-KO/ Xor- KO mice means urate derived from gut microbiota is absorbed by the intestine. Likewise, over excretion of oxypurine in mice may be caused by intestinal absorption of oxypurine. For nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + replenishment, oral supplementation with 1% L-tryptophan, an alternative precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , resulted in a recovery of body weight gain in high-HPRT/Uox-KO/ Xor- KO mice. In conclusion, the death of Xor -KO mice by renal failure seems to be caused by a depletion in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels due to the intracellular accumulation of hypoxanthine. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + replenishment by oral supplementation of nicotinamide mononucleotide or tryptophan was complicated by the effect of gut microbiota and failed to rescue high-HPRT/ Xor -KO mice. The attenuation of intestinal absorption of oxypurines seems to be necessary to avoid hypoxanthine accumulation and over excretion of oxypurine.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide⁺ in aging, metabolism, and neurodegeneration.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)) is a coenzyme found in all living cells. It serves both as a critical coenzyme for enzymes that fuel reduction-oxidation reactions, carrying electrons from one reaction to another, and as a cosubstrate for other enzymes such as the sirtuins and poly(adenosine diphosphate-ribose) polymerases. Cellular nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) concentrations change during aging, and modulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) usage or production can prolong both health span and life span. Here we review factors that regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) and discuss how supplementation with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) precursors may represent a new therapeutic opportunity for aging and its associated disorders, particularly neurodegenerative diseases.
sentences: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)) is a coenzyme found in all living cells.

It serves both as a critical coenzyme for enzymes that fuel reduction-oxidation reactions, carrying electrons from one reaction to another, and as a cosubstrate for other enzymes such as the sirtuins and poly(adenosine diphosphate-ribose) polymerases.

Cellular nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) concentrations change during aging, and modulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) usage or production can prolong both health span and life span.

Here we review factors that regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) and discuss how supplementation with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) precursors may represent a new therapeutic opportunity for aging and its associated disorders, particularly neurodegenerative diseases.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism: Role in senescence regulation and aging.
abstract: The geroscience hypothesis proposes that addressing the biology of aging could directly prevent the onset or mitigate the severity of multiple chronic diseases. Understanding the interplay between key aspects of the biological hallmarks of aging is essential in delivering the promises of the geroscience hypothesis. Notably, the nucleotide nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) interfaces with several biological hallmarks of aging, including cellular senescence, and changes in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism have been shown to be involved in the aging process. The relationship between nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism and cellular senescence appears to be complex. On the one hand, the accumulation of DNA damage and mitochondrial dysfunction induced by low nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + can promote the development of senescence. On the other hand, the low nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + state that occurs during aging may inhibit SASP development as this secretory phenotype and the development of cellular senescence are both highly metabolically demanding. However, to date, the impact of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism on the progression of the cellular senescence phenotype has not been fully characterized. Therefore, to explore the implications of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide replacement therapies, it is essential to consider their interactions with other hallmarks of aging, including cellular senescence. We propose that a comprehensive understanding of the interplay between nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide boosting strategies and senolytic agents is necessary to advance the field.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism and its roles in cellular processes during ageing.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) is a coenzyme for redox reactions, making it central to energy metabolism. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + is also an essential cofactor for non-redox nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent enzymes, including sirtuins, CD38 and poly(ADP-ribose) polymerases. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + can directly and indirectly influence many key cellular functions, including metabolic pathways, DNA repair, chromatin remodelling, cellular senescence and immune cell function. These cellular processes and functions are critical for maintaining tissue and metabolic homeostasis and for healthy ageing. Remarkably, ageing is accompanied by a gradual decline in tissue and cellular nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels in multiple model organisms, including rodents and humans. This decline in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels is linked causally to numerous ageing-associated diseases, including cognitive decline, cancer, metabolic disease, sarcopenia and frailty. Many of these ageing-associated diseases can be slowed down and even reversed by restoring nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels. Therefore, targeting nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism has emerged as a potential therapeutic approach to ameliorate ageing-related disease, and extend the human healthspan and lifespan. However, much remains to be learnt about how nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + influences human health and ageing biology. This includes a deeper understanding of the molecular mechanisms that regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels, how to effectively restore nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels during ageing, whether doing so is safe and whether nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + repletion will have beneficial effects in ageing humans.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + Metabolism in Cardiac Health, Aging, and Disease.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) is a central metabolite involved in energy and redox homeostasis as well as in DNA repair and protein deacetylation reactions. Pharmacological or genetic inhibition of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -degrading enzymes, external supplementation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors, and transgenic overexpression of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -generating enzymes have wide positive effects on metabolic health and age-associated diseases. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + pools tend to decline with normal aging, obesity, and hypertension, which are all major risk factors for cardiovascular disease, and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + replenishment extends healthspan, avoids metabolic syndrome, and reduces blood pressure in preclinical models. In addition, experimental elevation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + improves atherosclerosis, ischemic, diabetic, arrhythmogenic, hypertrophic, or dilated cardiomyopathies, as well as different modalities of heart failure. Here, we critically discuss cardiomyocyte-specific circuitries of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism, comparatively evaluate distinct nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors for their preclinical efficacy, and raise outstanding questions on the optimal design of clinical trials in which nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + replenishment or supraphysiological nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + elevations are assessed for the prevention or treatment of major cardiac diseases. We surmise that patients with hitherto intractable cardiac diseases such as heart failure with preserved ejection fraction may profit from the administration of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors. The development of such nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -centered treatments will rely on technological and conceptual progress on the fine regulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ and sirtuins in aging and disease.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)) is a classical coenzyme mediating many redox reactions. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) also plays an important role in the regulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)-consuming enzymes, including sirtuins, poly-ADP-ribose polymerases (PARPs), and CD38/157 ectoenzymes. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) biosynthesis, particularly mediated by nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT), and SIRT1 function together to regulate metabolism and circadian rhythm. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) levels decline during the aging process and may be an Achilles' heel, causing defects in nuclear and mitochondrial functions and resulting in many age-associated pathologies. Restoring nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) by supplementing nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) intermediates can dramatically ameliorate these age-associated functional defects, counteracting many diseases of aging, including neurodegenerative diseases. Thus, the combination of sirtuin activation and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) intermediate supplementation may be an effective antiaging intervention, providing hope to aging societies worldwide.
sentences: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)) is a classical coenzyme mediating many redox reactions. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) also plays an important role in the regulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)-consuming enzymes, including sirtuins, poly-ADP-ribose polymerases (PARPs), and CD38/157 ectoenzymes. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) biosynthesis, particularly mediated by nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT), and SIRT1 function together to regulate metabolism and circadian rhythm. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) levels decline during the aging process and may be an Achilles' heel, causing defects in nuclear and mitochondrial functions and resulting in many age-associated pathologies.

Restoring nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) by supplementing nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) intermediates can dramatically ameliorate these age-associated functional defects, counteracting many diseases of aging, including neurodegenerative diseases.

Thus, the combination of sirtuin activation and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) intermediate supplementation may be an effective antiaging intervention, providing hope to aging societies worldwide.

title: Regulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in aging and disease.
abstract: More than a century after discovering nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , information is still evolving on the role of this molecule in health and diseases. The biological functions of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors encompass pathways in cellular energetics, inflammation, metabolism, and cell survival. Several metabolic and neurological diseases exhibit reduced tissue nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels. Significantly reduced levels of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + are also associated with aging, and enhancing nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels improved healthspan and lifespan in animal models. Recent studies suggest a causal link between senescence, age-associated reduction in tissue nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and enzymatic degradation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + . Furthermore, the discovery of transporters and receptors involved in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursor (nicotinic acid, or niacin, nicotinamide, and nicotinamide riboside) metabolism allowed for a better understanding of their role in cellular homeostasis including signaling functions that are independent of their functions in redox reactions. We also review studies that demonstrate that the functional effect of niacin is partially due to the activation of its cell surface receptor, GPR109a. Based on the recent progress in understanding the mechanism and function of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors in cell metabolism, new strategies are evolving to exploit these molecules' pharmacological potential in the maintenance of metabolic balance.

Several metabolic and neurological diseases exhibit reduced tissue nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels.

We also review studies that demonstrate that the functional effect of niacin is partially due to the activation of its cell surface receptor, GPR109a.

Based on the recent progress in understanding the mechanism and function of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors in cell metabolism, new strategies are evolving to exploit these molecules' pharmacological potential in the maintenance of metabolic balance.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism: Implications in aging and longevity.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is an important co-factor involved in numerous physiological processes, including metabolism, post-translational protein modification, and DNA repair. In living organisms, a careful balance between nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide production and degradation serves to regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels. Recently, a number of studies have demonstrated that nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels decrease with age, and the deterioration of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism promotes several aging-associated diseases, including metabolic and neurodegenerative diseases and various cancers. Conversely, the upregulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism, including dietary supplementation with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide precursors, has been shown to prevent the decline of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide and exhibits beneficial effects against aging and aging-associated diseases. In addition, many studies have demonstrated that genetic and/or nutritional activation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism can extend the lifespan of diverse organisms. Collectively, it is clear that nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism plays important roles in aging and longevity. In this review, we summarize the basic functions of the enzymes involved in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide synthesis and degradation, as well as the outcomes of their dysregulation in various aging processes. In addition, a particular focus is given on the role of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism in the longevity of various organisms, with a discussion of the remaining obstacles in this research field.

In living organisms, a careful balance between nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide production and degradation serves to regulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels.

Recently, a number of studies have demonstrated that nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels decrease with age, and the deterioration of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism promotes several aging-associated diseases, including metabolic and neurodegenerative diseases and various cancers.

title: [nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism as a target for anti-aging].
abstract: Aging is a physiological process caused by various genetic and environmental factors. Recently, it has been proposed that the disturbance of the nutritional-metabolic sensing pathway is one of the aging characteristics. In particular, nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) plays an important role in this pathway and is considered the regulator of aging. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + regulates an energy metabolism as a co-factor and is also involved in various biological processes including transcription, stress responses, DNA repair, inflammatory responses as well as post-transcriptional modifications, as a substrate for sirtuins, poly ADP-ribose polymerase (PARP), and CD38. With age, DNA damage and chronic inflammation increase in organs, resulting in overconsumption of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + via PARP and CD38. The reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels decrease the activity of sirtuins and PARPs and impair energy metabolism, ultimately leading to aging and aging-related diseases. However, the precise metabolism of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in vivo and the mechanism of how nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + regulates aging remain elusive. Moreover, the clinical application of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + supplementation therapy is still under development. In this review, we overview the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism and its relation to aging. In addition, we describe the current issue and perspective of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + supplementation therapy to promote a healthy lifespan.

In this review, we overview the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism and its relation to aging.
In addition, we describe the current issue and perspective of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + supplementation therapy to promote a healthy lifespan.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + rescues aging-induced blood-brain barrier damage via the CX43-PARP1 axis.
abstract: Blood-brain barrier (BBB) function deteriorates during aging, contributing to cognitive impairment and neurodegeneration. It is unclear what drives BBB leakage in aging and how it can be prevented. Using single-nucleus transcriptomics, we identified decreased connexin 43 (CX43) expression in cadherin-5 + (Cdh5 + ) cerebral vascular cells in naturally aging mice and confirmed it in human brain samples. Global or Cdh5 + cell-specific CX43 deletion in mice exacerbated BBB dysfunction during aging. The CX43-dependent effect was not due to its canonical gap junction function but was associated with reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and mitochondrial dysfunction through nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent sirtuin 3 (SIRT3). CX43 interacts with and negatively regulates poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP1). Pharmacologic inhibition of PARP1 by olaparib or nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation rescued nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and alleviated aging-associated BBB leakage. These findings establish the endothelial CX43-PARP1-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + pathway's role in vascular aging and identify a potential therapeutic strategy to combat aging-associated BBB leakage with neuroprotective implications.
sentences: Blood-brain barrier (BBB) function deteriorates during aging, contributing to cognitive impairment and neurodegeneration.
It is unclear what drives BBB leakage in aging and how it can be prevented.

Using single-nucleus transcriptomics, we identified decreased connexin 43 (CX43) expression in cadherin-5 + (Cdh5 + ) cerebral vascular cells in naturally aging mice and confirmed it in human brain samples.
Global or Cdh5 + cell-specific CX43 deletion in mice exacerbated BBB dysfunction during aging.

The CX43-dependent effect was not due to its canonical gap junction function but was associated with reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and mitochondrial dysfunction through nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent sirtuin 3 (SIRT3).
CX43 interacts with and negatively regulates poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP1).

Pharmacologic inhibition of PARP1 by olaparib or nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) supplementation rescued nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and alleviated aging-associated BBB leakage.

These findings establish the endothelial CX43-PARP1-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + pathway's role in vascular aging and identify a potential therapeutic strategy to combat aging-associated BBB leakage with neuroprotective implications.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism: pathophysiologic mechanisms and therapeutic potential.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) and its metabolites function as critical regulators to maintain physiologic processes, enabling the plastic cells to adapt to environmental changes including nutrient perturbation, genotoxic factors, circadian disorder, infection, inflammation and xenobiotics. These effects are mainly achieved by the driving effect of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + on metabolic pathways as enzyme cofactors transferring hydrogen in oxidation-reduction reactions. Besides, multiple nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent enzymes are involved in physiology either by post-synthesis chemical modification of DNA, RNA and proteins, or releasing second messenger cyclic ADP-ribose (cADPR) and NAADP + . Prolonged disequilibrium of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism disturbs the physiological functions, resulting in diseases including metabolic diseases, cancer, aging and neurodegeneration disorder. In this review, we summarize recent advances in our understanding of the molecular mechanisms of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -regulated physiological responses to stresses, the contribution of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + deficiency to various diseases via manipulating cellular communication networks and the potential new avenues for therapeutic intervention.
sentences: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) and its metabolites function as critical regulators to maintain physiologic processes, enabling the plastic cells to adapt to environmental changes including nutrient perturbation, genotoxic factors, circadian disorder, infection, inflammation and xenobiotics.
These effects are mainly achieved by the driving effect of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + on metabolic pathways as enzyme cofactors transferring hydrogen in oxidation-reduction reactions.

Besides, multiple nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent enzymes are involved in physiology either by post-synthesis chemical modification of DNA, RNA and proteins, or releasing second messenger cyclic ADP-ribose (cADPR) and NAADP + .

In this review, we summarize recent advances in our understanding of the molecular mechanisms of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -regulated physiological responses to stresses, the contribution of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + deficiency to various diseases via manipulating cellular communication networks and the potential new avenues for therapeutic intervention.

title: Implications of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + Metabolism in the Aging Retina and Retinal Degeneration.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) plays an important role in various key biological processes including energy metabolism, DNA repair, and gene expression. Accumulating clinical and experimental evidence highlights an age-dependent decline in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and its association with the development and progression of several age-related diseases. This supports the establishment of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + as a critical regulator of aging and longevity and, relatedly, a promising therapeutic target to counter adverse events associated with the normal process of aging and/or the development and progression of age-related disease. Relative to the above, the metabolism of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + has been the subject of numerous investigations in various cells, tissues, and organ systems; however, interestingly, studies of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in the retina and its relevance to the regulation of visual health and function are comparatively few. This is surprising given the critical causative impact of mitochondrial oxidative damage and bioenergetic crises on the development and progression of degenerative disease of the retina. Hence, the role of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in this tissue, normally and aging and/or disease, should not be ignored. Herein, we discuss important findings in the field of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism, with particular emphasis on the importance of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + biosynthesizing enzyme NAMPT, the related metabolism of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in the retina, and the consequences of NAMPT and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + deficiency or depletion in this tissue in aging and disease. We discuss also the implications of potential therapeutic strategies that augment nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels on the preservation of retinal health and function in the above conditions. The overarching goal of this review is to emphasize the importance of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in normal, aging, and/or diseased retina and, by so doing, highlight the necessity of additional clinical studies dedicated to evaluating the therapeutic utility of strategies that enhance nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels in improving vision.
sentences: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) plays an important role in various key biological processes including energy metabolism, DNA repair, and gene expression.

Accumulating clinical and experimental evidence highlights an age-dependent decline in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and its association with the development and progression of several age-related diseases.

This supports the establishment of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + as a critical regulator of aging and longevity and, relatedly, a promising therapeutic target to counter adverse events associated with the normal process of aging and/or the development and progression of age-related disease.

Relative to the above, the metabolism of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + has been the subject of numerous investigations in various cells, tissues, and organ systems; however, interestingly, studies of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in the retina and its relevance to the regulation of visual health and function are comparatively few.
This is surprising given the critical causative impact of mitochondrial oxidative damage and bioenergetic crises on the development and progression of degenerative disease of the retina.
Hence, the role of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in this tissue, normally and aging and/or disease, should not be ignored.

Herein, we discuss important findings in the field of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism, with particular emphasis on the importance of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + biosynthesizing enzyme NAMPT, the related metabolism of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in the retina, and the consequences of NAMPT and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + deficiency or depletion in this tissue in aging and disease.

We discuss also the implications of potential therapeutic strategies that augment nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels on the preservation of retinal health and function in the above conditions.

The overarching goal of this review is to emphasize the importance of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in normal, aging, and/or diseased retina and, by so doing, highlight the necessity of additional clinical studies dedicated to evaluating the therapeutic utility of strategies that enhance nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels in improving vision.

title: Pathobiochemistry of Aging and Neurodegeneration: Deregulation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ Metabolism in Brain Cells.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ plays a pivotal role in energy metabolism and adaptation to external stimuli and stressful conditions. A significant reduction in intracellular nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ levels is associated with aging and contributes to the development of chronic cardiovascular, neurodegenerative, and metabolic diseases. It is of particular importance to maintain optimal levels of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ in cells with high energy consumption, particularly in the brain. Maintaining the tissue level of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ with pharmacological tools has the potential to slow down the aging process, to prevent the development of age-related diseases. This review covers key aspects of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ metabolism in terms of brain metabolic plasticity, including nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ biosynthesis and degradation in different types of brain cells, as well as its contribution to the development of neurodegeneration and aging, and highlights up-to-date approaches to modulate nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide+ levels in brain cells.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism governs the proinflammatory senescence-associated secretome.
abstract: Cellular senescence is a stable growth arrest that is implicated in tissue ageing and cancer. Senescent cells are characterized by an upregulation of proinflammatory cytokines, which is termed the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP). nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism influences both tissue ageing and cancer. However, the role of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism in regulating the SASP is poorly understood. Here, we show that nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT), the rate-limiting enzyme of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + salvage pathway, governs the proinflammatory SASP independent of senescence-associated growth arrest. NAMPT expression is regulated by high mobility group A (HMGA) proteins during senescence. The HMGA-NAMPT-nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + signalling axis promotes the proinflammatory SASP by enhancing glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration. HMGA proteins and NAMPT promote the proinflammatory SASP through nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -mediated suppression of AMPK kinase, which suppresses the p53-mediated inhibition of p38 MAPK to enhance NF-κB activity. We conclude that nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism governs the proinflammatory SASP. Given the tumour-promoting effects of the proinflammatory SASP, our results suggest that anti-ageing dietary nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + augmentation should be administered with precision.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in Brain Aging and Neurodegenerative Disorders.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + is a pivotal metabolite involved in cellular bioenergetics, genomic stability, mitochondrial homeostasis, adaptive stress responses, and cell survival. Multiple nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent enzymes are involved in synaptic plasticity and neuronal stress resistance. Here, we review emerging findings that reveal key roles for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and related metabolites in the adaptation of neurons to a wide range of physiological stressors and in counteracting processes in neurodegenerative diseases, such as those occurring in Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington diseases, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Advances in understanding the molecular and cellular mechanisms of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -based neuronal resilience will lead to novel approaches for facilitating healthy brain aging and for the treatment of a range of neurological disorders.
sentences: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + is a pivotal metabolite involved in cellular bioenergetics, genomic stability, mitochondrial homeostasis, adaptive stress responses, and cell survival.
Multiple nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent enzymes are involved in synaptic plasticity and neuronal stress resistance.

Here, we review emerging findings that reveal key roles for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and related metabolites in the adaptation of neurons to a wide range of physiological stressors and in counteracting processes in neurodegenerative diseases, such as those occurring in Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington diseases, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

title: CD38 ecto-enzyme in immune cells is induced during aging and regulates nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and nicotinamide mononucleotide levels.
abstract: Decreased nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels have been shown to contribute to metabolic dysfunction during aging. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + decline can be partially prevented by knockout of the enzyme CD38. However, it is not known how CD38 is regulated during aging, and how its ecto-enzymatic activity impacts nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + homeostasis. Here we show that an increase in CD38 in white adipose tissue (WAT) and the liver during aging is mediated by accumulation of CD38 + immune cells. Inflammation increases CD38 and decreases nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + . In addition, senescent cells and their secreted signals promote accumulation of CD38 + cells in WAT, and ablation of senescent cells or their secretory phenotype decreases CD38, partially reversing nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + decline. Finally, blocking the ecto-enzymatic activity of CD38 can increase nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + through a nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide)-dependent process. Our findings demonstrate that senescence-induced inflammation promotes accumulation of CD38 in immune cells that, through its ecto-enzymatic activity, decreases levels of nicotinamide mononucleotide and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + .

title: Roles of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in Health and Aging.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , the essential metabolite involved in multiple reactions such as the regulation of cellular metabolism, energy production, DNA repair, mitophagy and autophagy, inflammation, and neuronal function, has been the subject of intense research in the field of aging and disease over the last decade. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels decline with aging and in some age-related diseases, and reduction in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + affects all the hallmarks of aging. Here, we present an overview of the discovery of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , the cellular pathways of producing and consuming nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , and discuss how imbalances in the production rate and cellular request of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + likely contribute to aging and age-related diseases including neurodegeneration. Preclinical studies have revealed great potential for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors in promotion of healthy aging and improvement of neurodegeneration. This has led to the initiation of several clinical trials with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + precursors to treat accelerated aging, age-associated dysfunctions, and diseases including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide supplementation has great future potential clinically, and these studies will also provide insight into the mechanisms of aging.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) Metabolism and the Control of Energy Homeostasis: A Balancing Act between Mitochondria and the Nucleus.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) has emerged as a vital cofactor that can rewire metabolism, activate sirtuins, and maintain mitochondrial fitness through mechanisms such as the mitochondrial unfolded protein response. This improved understanding of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) metabolism revived interest in nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+)-boosting strategies to manage a wide spectrum of diseases, ranging from diabetes to cancer. In this review, we summarize how nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide(+) metabolism links energy status with adaptive cellular and organismal responses and how this knowledge can be therapeutically exploited.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism in aging and cancer.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide is a central metabolite connecting energy balance and organismal growth with genomic integrity and function. It is involved in the development of malignancy and has a regulatory role in the aging process. These processes are mediated by a diverse series of enzymes whose common focus is either nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide's biosynthesis or its utilization as a redox cofactor or enzyme substrate. These enzymes include dehydrogenases, cyclic ADP-ribose hydrolases, mono(ADP-ribosyl)transferases, poly(ADP-ribose) polymerases, and sirtuin deacetylases. This article describes the manifold pathways that comprise nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism and promotes an increased awareness of how perturbations in these systems may be important in disease prevention and/or progression.

It is involved in the development of malignancy and has a regulatory role in the aging process.
These processes are mediated by a diverse series of enzymes whose common focus is either nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide's biosynthesis or its utilization as a redox cofactor or enzyme substrate.
These enzymes include dehydrogenases, cyclic ADP-ribose hydrolases, mono(ADP-ribosyl)transferases, poly(ADP-ribose) polymerases, and sirtuin deacetylases.

This article describes the manifold pathways that comprise nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism and promotes an increased awareness of how perturbations in these systems may be important in disease prevention and/or progression.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + Repletion Rescues Female Fertility during Reproductive Aging.
abstract: Reproductive aging in female mammals is an irreversible process associated with declining oocyte quality, which is the rate-limiting factor to fertility. Here, we show that this loss of oocyte quality with age accompanies declining levels of the prominent metabolic cofactor nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ). Treatment with the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolic precursor nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide) rejuvenates oocyte quality in aged animals, leading to restoration in fertility, and this can be recapitulated by transgenic overexpression of the nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent deacylase SIRT2, though deletion of this enzyme does not impair oocyte quality. These benefits of nicotinamide mononucleotide extend to the developing embryo, where supplementation reverses the adverse effect of maternal age on developmental milestones. These findings suggest that late-life restoration of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels represents an opportunity to rescue female reproductive function in mammals.

title: An nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent metabolic checkpoint regulates hematopoietic stem cell activation and aging.
abstract: How hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) maintain metabolic homeostasis to support tissue repair and regeneration throughout the lifespan is elusive. Here, we show that CD38, an nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent metabolic enzyme, promotes HSC proliferation by inducing mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx and mitochondrial metabolism in young mice. Conversely, aberrant CD38 upregulation during aging is a driver of HSC deterioration in aged mice due to dysregulated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism and compromised mitochondrial stress management. The mitochondrial calcium uniporter, a mediator of mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx, also supports HSC proliferation in young mice yet drives HSC decline in aged mice. Pharmacological inactivation of CD38 reverses HSC aging and the pathophysiological changes of the aging hematopoietic system in aged mice. Together, our study highlights an nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolic checkpoint that balances mitochondrial activation to support HSC proliferation and mitochondrial stress management to enhance HSC self-renewal throughout the lifespan, and links aberrant Ca 2+ signaling to HSC aging.

Here, we show that CD38, an nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent metabolic enzyme, promotes HSC proliferation by inducing mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx and mitochondrial metabolism in young mice.

Conversely, aberrant CD38 upregulation during aging is a driver of HSC deterioration in aged mice due to dysregulated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + metabolism and compromised mitochondrial stress management.
The mitochondrial calcium uniporter, a mediator of mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx, also supports HSC proliferation in young mice yet drives HSC decline in aged mice.

title: Nicotinic acid riboside maintains nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + homeostasis and ameliorates aging-associated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + decline.
abstract: Liver-derived circulating nicotinamide from nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) catabolism primarily feeds systemic organs for nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + synthesis. We surprisingly found that, despite blunted hepatic nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + and nicotinamide production in liver-specific nicotinamide nucleotide adenylyltransferase 1 (NMNAT1) deletion mice (liver-specific knockout [LKO]), circulating nicotinamide and extra-hepatic organs' nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + are unaffected. Metabolomics reveals a massive accumulation of a novel molecule in the LKO liver, which we identify as nicotinic acid riboside (NaR). We further demonstrate cytosolic 5'-nucleotidase II (NT5C2) as the NaR-producing enzyme. The liver releases NaR to the bloodstream, and kidneys take up NaR to synthesize nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + through nicotinamide riboside kinase 1 (NRK1) and replenish circulating nicotinamide. Serum NaR levels decline with aging, whereas oral NaR supplementation in aged mice boosts serum nicotinamide and multi-organ nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , including kidneys, and reduces kidney inflammation and albuminuria. Thus, the liver-kidney axis maintains systemic nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + homeostasis via circulating NaR, and NaR supplement ameliorates aging-associated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + decline and kidney dysfunction.

title: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide depletion in skeletal muscle does not compromise muscle function or accelerate aging.
abstract: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide) is a ubiquitous electron carrier essential for energy metabolism and post-translational modification of numerous regulatory proteins. Dysregulations of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide metabolism are widely regarded as detrimental to health, with nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide depletion commonly implicated in aging. However, the extent to which cellular nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide concentration can decline without adverse consequences remains unclear. To investigate this, we generated a mouse model in which nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)-mediated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + biosynthesis was disrupted in adult skeletal muscle. The intervention resulted in an 85% reduction in muscle nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + abundance while maintaining tissue integrity and functionality, as demonstrated by preserved muscle morphology, contractility, and exercise tolerance. This absence of functional impairments was further supported by intact mitochondrial respiratory capacity and unaltered muscle transcriptomic and proteomic profiles. Furthermore, lifelong nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide depletion did not accelerate muscle aging or impair whole-body metabolism. Collectively, these findings suggest that nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide depletion does not contribute to age-related decline in skeletal muscle function.

To investigate this, we generated a mouse model in which nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT)-mediated nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + biosynthesis was disrupted in adult skeletal muscle.

title: Macrophage de novo nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + synthesis specifies immune function in aging and inflammation.
abstract: Recent advances highlight a pivotal role for cellular metabolism in programming immune responses. Here, we demonstrate that cell-autonomous generation of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + ) via the kynurenine pathway (KP) regulates macrophage immune function in aging and inflammation. Isotope tracer studies revealed that macrophage nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + derives substantially from KP metabolism of tryptophan. Genetic or pharmacological blockade of de novo nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + synthesis depleted nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , suppressed mitochondrial nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + -dependent signaling and respiration, and impaired phagocytosis and resolution of inflammation. Innate immune challenge triggered upstream KP activation but paradoxically suppressed cell-autonomous nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + synthesis by limiting the conversion of downstream quinolinate to nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + , a profile recapitulated in aging macrophages. Increasing de novo nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + generation in immune-challenged or aged macrophages restored oxidative phosphorylation and homeostatic immune responses. Thus, KP-derived nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + operates as a metabolic switch to specify macrophage effector responses. Breakdown of de novo nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + synthesis may underlie declining nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + levels and rising innate immune dysfunction in aging and age-associated diseases.
sentences: Recent advances highlight a pivotal role for cellular metabolism in programming immune responses.

title: Long-Term Administration of Nicotinamide Mononucleotide Mitigates Age-Associated Physiological Decline in Mice.
abstract: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + availability decreases with age and in certain disease conditions. Nicotinamide mononucleotide (nicotinamide mononucleotide), a key nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + intermediate, has been shown to enhance nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + biosynthesis and ameliorate various pathologies in mouse disease models. In this study, we conducted a 12-month-long nicotinamide mononucleotide administration to regular chow-fed wild-type C57BL/6N mice during their normal aging. Orally administered nicotinamide mononucleotide was quickly utilized to synthesize nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + in tissues. Remarkably, nicotinamide mononucleotide effectively mitigates age-associated physiological decline in mice. Without any obvious toxicity or deleterious effects, nicotinamide mononucleotide suppressed age-associated body weight gain, enhanced energy metabolism, promoted physical activity, improved insulin sensitivity and plasma lipid profile, and ameliorated eye function and other pathophysiologies. Consistent with these phenotypes, nicotinamide mononucleotide prevented age-associated gene expression changes in key metabolic organs and enhanced mitochondrial oxidative metabolism and mitonuclear protein imbalance in skeletal muscle. These effects of nicotinamide mononucleotide highlight the preventive and therapeutic potential of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + intermediates as effective anti-aging interventions in humans.
sentences: nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide + availability decreases with age and in certain disease conditions.
